title: Setting up a bokashi bin
subtitle: Ferment cooked food and meat scraps in an airtight bucket, then bury in the garden
excerpt: Bokashi uses anaerobic fermentation rather than decomposition. An airtight bucket, bokashi bran (inoculated with effective microorganisms), and a drain tap for leachate are the only components. The fermented material buries in the garden in two weeks and breaks down quickly. Accepts cooked meat, fish, and dairy, materials that cannot go in a standard compost heap.
sourceNotes: Bokashi Living and TerraCycle bokashi system instructions. WRAP food waste composting alternatives guidance. Academic basis: Teraoka et al. 2003 on EM fermentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Japanese composting method using anaerobic fermentation with effective microorganisms (EM). The material is fermented rather than decomposed — it leaves the bucket in a pickled state and completes breakdown quickly once buried in soil.

The liquid that drains from a composting system. Bokashi leachate is acidic and nutrient-rich; dilute 1:100 with water before using as a liquid plant feed, or pour neat down drains as a drain cleaner.

content.text: differs from all other composting methods in that it operates anaerobically, no air enters the bucket. This means it accepts cooked food, meat, fish, and dairy, which would attract rats in an open heap and cannot go in a standard compost bin. The fermented material is not ready compost, it is a pre-digested soil amendment that finishes breaking down in two to four weeks after burial.
Bokashi bin, an airtight bucket with a false floor and a drain tap near the base. Sold in pairs so one fills while the other ferments.
Bokashi bran, wheat bran or sawdust inoculated with effective microorganisms. Sold in resealable bags. A standard 1 kg bag lasts approximately two months of regular use.

Add food scraps in layers. After each addition, scatter a thin layer of bokashi bran (a tablespoon per 100 g of waste is the usual ratio) and press down firmly to exclude air. Liquids should be minimal, drain excess from cooked food before adding.
Step 2, seal the lid after every addition.
The process fails if air enters. Replace the lid firmly each time.
from the tap every 2-3 days. Leachate builds up quickly and inhibits the process if left undrained. Dilute 1:100 with water as a liquid feed, or pour neat down drains.

Once the bin is full, seal it for two weeks at room temperature. Do not open during this period. A well-fermented bin smells pleasantly sour-pickled; a failed fermentation smells of rot. After two weeks, bury the contents 20-30 cm deep in soil or add to a compost heap.

items.symptom: Bin smells of rot, not vinegar
items.cause: Insufficient bran, or air entering the bin
items.fix: Increase bran to 2 tablespoons per addition. Check the lid seal and drain tap are both airtight. If the batch has failed, bury it anyway, it will still break down in soil.

items.cause: Normal surface growth of the effective microorganisms
items.fix: No action needed. White surface mould is expected and indicates active fermentation.

items.cause: Air exposure causing unwanted mould contamination
items.fix: Discard the batch and check the bin lid for a damaged seal before starting again.
